coredata.dc:title: Sensitive fluorescence detection of atorvastatin by doped carbon dots synthesized in deep eutectic media
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Nazari, Fereshteh
Tabaraki, Reza
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence properties of nanoparticles can be influenced by solvent. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized in deep eutectic solvent by microwave assisted method. Quantum yield (QY) and size of the synthesized CDs were 41.3% and 2 nm, respectively. N/Cl –doped CDs had excellent sensitivity and selectivity for atorvastatin and detection limit was 0.8 nM. Simple and low-cost synthesis method and excellent sensitivity are advantages of this detection method for atorvastatin. The as-synthesized N/Cl-doped CDs were successfully used to determine atorvastatin in blood serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Microwave
Atorvastatin
Deep eutectic solvent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the CDs (left) and size distribution histogram (right).
FT-IR spectrum (A) and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (B).
Relative fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of various ions and molecules.
A) Effect of time, and B) effect of pH on the florescence intensity in the presence of 10 × 10−6 M atorvastatin.
(A) Stern-Volmer plot at various concentration of atorvastatin (0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.5, 1 μM), and (B) Stern-Volmer plot at 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 25, 30, 40, 50 μM.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of N/Cl-CDs and N/Cl-CDs + Atorvastatin.
Comparison of different methods for atorvastatin determination.
Quenching constants for the interaction of the CDs with atorvastatin.

head.ce:title: Sensitive fluorescence detection of atorvastatin by doped carbon dots synthesized in deep eutectic media

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence properties of nanoparticles can be influenced by solvent. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized in deep eutectic solvent by microwave assisted method. Quantum yield (QY) and size of the synthesized CDs were 41.3% and 2 nm, respectively. N/Cl –doped CDs had excellent sensitivity and selectivity for atorvastatin and detection limit was 0.8 nM. Simple and low-cost synthesis method and excellent sensitivity are advantages of this detection method for atorvastatin. The as-synthesized N/Cl-doped CDs were successfully used to determine atorvastatin in blood serum.

Carbon dots synthesize and atorvastatin detection.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are nanomaterials with good solubility in water, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and photobleaching resistance. Their applications in sensing, imaging, and catalysis need new methods of synthesis and functionalization [1–3]. Among different bottom-top approaches for the preparation of fluorescent CDs, the microwave-assisted approach is a fast, one step, and low cost synthesis method. CDs prepared from different simple and inexpensive synthesis methods have low fluorescence quantum yields (QYs <10%). This disadvantage limits their applications, but doping with heteroatoms such as N, S, and P enhances CDs quantum yield [4]. Nitrogen is the most prominent doping candidate for fluorescent CDs. Generally, the carbon source and nitrogen source are used together in the microwave method [5,6].

Deep eutectic solvents (DESs) are newly emerged solvents with unique properties such as low-cost, non-toxic, biodegradable, and recyclable [7]. DES formation needs to hydrogen bonding. Therefore, a hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) and a hydrogen bond donor (HBD) are mixed at a proper molar eutectic ratio and heated [8]. These green solvents are used in extraction [9,10] and chromatography [11]. Doped carbon dots were also synthesized in DESs and used for sensing and live-cell imaging [12–20].

HMG CoA reductase inhibitors are a family of drugs and a member of this family, atorvastatin with the chemical formula (C66H68CaF2N4O10.3H2O, 1155.3 g/mol) is used in high cholesterol treatment [21]. This drug can lower the risk of heart attack in type 2 diabetes and coronary heart patients [22].

In this work, N/Cl-doped carbon dots were synthesized by microwave-assisted method from a mixture of choline chloride (HBA) and urea (HBD) as deep eutectic solvent and nitrogen source and glucose as carbon source. Then, these highly fluorescent CDs were used for atorvastatin detection in human blood serum based on turn off mechanism for the first time. Quantum yield and quenching mechanism were also studied.
Choline chloride, urea, glucose, and atorvastatin were analytical grade and obtained from Merck. Universal buffer (0.01 M) was used for pH adjustment.
Vertex 70 FT-IR, Varian Cary 300 Bio, Cary eclipse, and TEM, MC30 Philips were used for FT-IR, UV–Vis, fluorescence spectra and characterization of CDs, respectively.

A mixture of urea (HBD molecule) and choline chloride (HBA molecule) with a 2:1 M ratio was heated overnight with constant stirring. Then, the resulting mixture was heated to 100 °C. Finally, a colorless and homogeneous liquid (deep eutectic solvent) was formed [23].

0.1 g of glucose and 1 mL of deionized water were mixed with choline chloride-urea DESs (1:1 v/v) until no solid is observed [24]. A brown solid was obtained after microwave heating (5 min, 600 W) of the mixture. The resulting brown solid was dissolved in 20 mL water, filtered (by 0.2 μm filter), and centrifuged (15 min at 12000 rpm). The solution was evaporated until dryness for three days at room temperature and the resulting solid was dissolved in 10 mL water and used for further analysis (0.03 mg/mL).

Serum blood samples were taken from a non-drug user. 4.0 mL of acetonitrile was added to 1.0 mL of human plasma and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, diluted samples (50 times with universal buffer) were used for analysis [25].

Quantum yield was measured according to the reported method [26]. The reference standard was quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54 at 365 nm). Absorbance and fluorescence of reference and CDs solutions were then measured, and quantum yield was calculated as:
(1)
Q =
Q R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R
2
where subscript R denotes reference, Q is CDs fluorescence quantum yield, and η is the refractive index of solvent. I and A are the measured emission intensity and optical density, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy image shows that CDs had an average particle size of about 2 nm and narrow size distribution (Fig. 1 ). Carbon dots functional groups were studied by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2A). As shown, the assigned bands are 3500 cm−1 (–OH stretching vibration), and 1366 cm−1(C–O–H bending), 1472 cm−1(C–O–C asymmetric stretching), 1624 cm−1 (CO stretching), 1300 cm−1(CH2–Cl bending), and 1666–1717 cm−1 (CONH2 amide carbonyl) [27].

CDs exhibited the maximal emission at 423 nm when were excited at 290 nm (Fig. 2B). Absorption peaks at 232 and 293 nm are related to electron transitions from π (or n) to π* in CC, CN, CO and NH2 bonds [26].

CDs prepared in DES had high fluorescent quantum yield of 41.3% against reference fluorophore (quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 54%). This high quantum yield was obtained from passivation of CDs states by doped nitrogen atoms [28].

Different ions and molecules were added to CDs solution with a final concentration of 3 μM and then, fluorescence spectra were recorded at 423 nm (Fig. 3 ). Fluorescence intensity of CDs had negligible or less change in the presence of the most species except atorvastatin. Therefore, the developed sensor can be used for highly selective and reliable atorvastatin monitoring.

Time and pH of sample are two experimental variables that can affect fluorescence quenching of CDs. One important advantage for a new sensor is fast response time. Fluorescence quenching was fast after addition of atorvastatin at room temperature (Fig. 4A). Fig. 4B shows the influence of the pH on the fluorescence intensities when pH changes from 2 to 11. As shown, relative fluorescence was increased in pH range of 3–8 and had a tendency to decrease at higher pHs. Therefore, a pH of 7.4 was chosen as optimum pH. Dissociation of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs may be reason of this behavior. In high basic environments, these functional groups changes disrupt or prohibit the electronic transition of some defects in CDs [29–31].

The feasibility of the proposed method was evaluated for atorvastatin detection in human serum sample. Different concentrations of atorvastatin at high, medium and low concentration levels were added to pretreated plasmas (Table 2 ). Recoveries were in the range of 96.5% to 106%, suggesting the applicability of the proposed method for atorvastatin analysis in biological samples.

Atorvastatin quenches the CDs fluorescence by interaction with surfaces of CDs and change in electron-hole recombination and energy transfer [39,40]. If this interaction produces a nonfluorescent ground-state complex, then quenching mechanism is called static quenching. In this mechanism, temperature rise declines complex stability and static quenching reduces. Effect of temperature on atorvastatin-CDs interactions was studied at 278 K, 303 K, 323 K with the Stern–Volmer equation:
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
= 1 + Kq
τ 0
Q
where F0, F, [Q], τ0, Kq, and KSV are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of atorvastatin, atorvastatin concentration, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs, the quenching rate constant, and the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, respectively. Results are shown in Table 3 . Good R2 and KSV decrease with temperature increase may approve static quenching mechanism. UV–Vis spectrum of N/Cl-CDs has a strong absorption peak at 293 nm (Fig. 6 ). After the addition of atorvastatin, the absorption peak significantly decreases and blue-shifts to 240 nm. The atorvastatin may be have a strong affinity with the surface of N/Cl-CDs, and forms the complex with N/Cl-CDs and quenches the fluorescence. The change of the absorption spectrum of the CDs as result of the formation of the ground-state complex was reported previously [13].

Nitrogen and chloride doped carbon dots (N/Cl-CDs) were synthesized by microwave assisted method in deep eutectic solvent and used for atorvastatin nano-sensing. Linear dynamic range and limit of detection were 0.025–50 μM, and 0.8 nM, respectively. Quantum yield (QY) and size of the synthesized CDs were 41.3% and 2 nm, respectively. This sensor can be used as the fluorescence nanosensor for atorvastatin detection in blood.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Fereshteh Nazari:Writing - original draft, Investigation, Visualization, Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Formal analysis.Reza Tabaraki:Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
